retrieved_docs.title: Ageusia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The normal aging process can also lead to a decline in the taste perception, but complete taste loss is rare. A reduction in taste sensation among elderly patients is common because of age-related regression in taste cells, reduced production of saliva, and an individual’s inability to chew food completely correlates with tooth loss. [7] Also, geriatric patients are affected by ageusia due to polypharmacy, dietary deficiency, and as a secondary complication of some oral and systemic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The following are alterations in mouth structure leading to changes in lip approximation and increased salivary pooling and maceration at the labial commissures: The normal loss of skin turgor due to aging, smoking, or rapid weight loss Loss of vertical dimension of the face due to severe tooth wear, edentulous states, and mal-fitted dentures increasing overhang of the upper lip onto the lower one (overclosure) Retrognathic malocclusion Deepened furrowing of the skin dependent on the commissures (marionette lines) Conditions associated with enlarged lips such as oro-facial granulomatosis (OFG). Up to 20% of OFG patients suffer from AC, but Candida is usually not isolated from lesions Down syndrome: 25% of patients have AC due to macroglossia leading to tongue protrusion and drooling [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Also, before starting the treatment of completely edentulous patients, a thorough examination of the oral cavity must be done as inspection of the oral cavity can give us crucial information regarding the dryness of the oral mucosa. The hyposalivation may be due to some medication or any illness. The dentist and family physician should adopt the interprofessional team approach, especially for geriatric patients, although all patients will benefit from this paradigm. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Proper geriatric care requires an interprofessional approach involving specialists from different aspects of healthcare. Apart from the regular assessment of other body functions, oral health also requires assessment, and thus a dental practitioner should be involved. If the patient needs a denture and has inadequate ridge support, which is one reason for unstable dentures, the dentist may opt for some surgical procedure s to enhance the rehabilitative outcome. In such cases, teamwork between the dentist and the patient’s general physician is necessary as geriatric patients may be suffering from some systemic illness or may be on medication that precludes surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment and Management of Traumatic Injuries in Older Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Gastrointestinal
retrieved_docs.content: Poor dentition leads older patients to become edentulous, necessitating prosthetic dentures. Loss of the ability to chew foods can lead to poor nutritional intake. Salivary glands also atrophy, leading to decreased saliva production, which impairs the lubrication of the food bolus, making the process of deglutition more challenging. Transfer of the food bolus from the oropharynx to the esophagus is also impaired and can lead to aspiration as prior protective aerodigestive reflexes are often blunted or absent.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue Taste Buds -- Clinical Significance -- Aging
retrieved_docs.content: Taste perception declines with aging, with salty and bitter qualities most affected. Abnormalities in taste are common in older adults but often underreported. [25] The underlying mechanisms are multifactorial and include loss of taste buds, reduced salivary secretion, and polypharmacy. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Issues of Concern -- A. Patient Muscle Control and Coordination
retrieved_docs.content: A well-coordinated patient will usually manage even if the dentures are not retentive and stable; these patients are called “oral acrobats,” but this situation should not be taken for granted. Conversely, if the patient has poor muscle control, especially if complicated by conditions of senility, Parkinsonism, etc., even an ideal fit may still leave the patient in a situation where he or she cannot manage the dentures. Thus, patient education and training are vital.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The mandible, which holds the lower teeth, comprises the majority of the lower third of the maxillofacial skeleton and is of utmost functional importance. Complex mandibular movements are afforded by the masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid muscles, and temporomandibular joints. [1] Given that the maxilla is stationary, mastication is dependent on mandibular movement. In certain populations, masticatory function related to dental status is a significant determinant of nutritional status. Being an edentulous geriatric patient without complete dentures was shown to be an independent risk factor for malnutrition. [2] This suggests the importance of functional occlusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bony viscerocranial atrophy occurs in the 3rd and 4th decades of life. Decreased skeletal support can accentuate aging, especially since resorption is not even. Areas with more pronounced resorption include the infraorbital and pyriform maxillary areas, leading to midfacial deficiency. Osseocutaneous ligaments, including the zygomatic and mandibular, attach to the corresponding bone's periosteum and overlying skin. [9] The masseter ligaments span from the muscle to the skin. Weakening and decreased elasticity of these ligaments contribute to the ptotic effect of gravity.

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An unstable denture may result in reduced maximum bite forces. Due to disuse atrophy, the jaw muscles may weaken in such cases. [9] This situation is a vicious cycle as the resultant weakening of the jaw muscles may further reduce maximum bite forces during mastication. This effect is more pronounced in patients with long-term use of conventional dentures and unstable lower dentures. [10] An unstable denture will affect not only the masticatory efficiency but also the psychology of the patient. In patients, particularly those who are more active socially, unstable dentures may lead to social isolation due to embarrassment; this also affects the patient's diet and leads to malnutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Merkel Cells
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who wear complete dentures are known to decrease oral perceptibility due to the loss of periodontal ligament. Recently studies have raised the possibility that an increase in Merkel cells may help to partially compensate the loss of mechanoreception from the loss of periodontal ligament in such patients. Despite this, edentulous patients’ oral perceptibility will remain compromised [19] .

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Age is a well-documented, non-modifiable risk factor for periodontal disease and other health conditions. Older individuals tend to exhibit an exacerbated inflammatory response to plaque accumulation, with increased aggregation of inflammatory cells accelerating periodontal tissue destruction. Additionally, reduced dexterity in this population can further compromise oral hygiene, leading to higher plaque levels. Research indicates that individuals aged 60 to 90 experience greater clinical attachment loss (CAL) compared to those aged 50 or younger. [7] [21] [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Geriatric Care Special Needs Assessment -- Issues of Concern -- Nutrition
retrieved_docs.content: Up to 15% of the community-dwelling elderly population is affected by malnutrition, which is associated with a decline in physical health, such as poor wound healing, anemia, immune dysfunction, etc. Poor cognition, functional decline, and an overall increase in mortality. [20] [21] Besides organic causes (chronic medical conditions, malignancy, poor dentition, etc.), various psychosocial causes (depression, alcoholism, isolation, bereavement, etc.) also lead to malnutrition in the elderly. [22] Although there is no single laboratory screening test for this condition, clinical assessment, serial weights on clinic visits, and various validated tools such as Mini Nutritional Assessment (MNA) or Geriatric Nutrition Risk Index (GNRI) can be used for nutritional assessment in older patients. [23] [24] The elderly population at-risk as determined by one of these screening strategies should undergo further evaluation to determine the cause and initiate treatment such as optimizing chronic medical conditions, proper dentition, adequate assistance for physically or mentally disabled patients, and oral supplementation or enteral feeding as needed. [22] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Ageusia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc deficiency is also responsible for abnormalities in taste perception in an otherwise healthy person and cases of drug-induced taste disorders. It can also result from some local injury and inflammation in the surrounding structure. The damage can be a result of burns, lacerations, surgery, and local anesthesia).  Taste function can also be affected by local antiplaque medicaments that get excreted into saliva, some infections (dentoalveolar, periodontal, and soft tissue infections), vesiculobullous conditions, complete and partial removable prostheses, metallic dental restorations, and dysfunction of the salivary gland.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Non-modifiable risk factors for gingivitis and associated periodontal disease include age and genetic disorders. The literature shows that the inflammatory response to plaque deposition in older individuals contains a significantly higher number of inflammatory cells than in younger individuals. Additionally, aging increases the risk of inadequate oral hygiene practices, further contributing to the increased risk of gingivitis and periodontal disease in the older population. Finally, genetic disorders such as Down syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and Crohn disease have proven to increase the risk of gingival disease. [16] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Health Considerations for Patients With Systemic Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures may appear routine, but they often involve irreversible surgical interventions to hard and soft oral tissues, underscoring the inherently invasive nature of general dental practice. Although these procedures are typically less physically traumatic compared to many medical surgeries, they still carry potential for systemic consequences. [1] A combination of aging population demographics, people retaining teeth for longer and later in life, and an increase in health conditions that can be surgically or medically managed results in a growing population requiring dental care in the background of systemic disease. The increasing prevalence and incidence of systemic diseases increases the need for a comprehensive risk assessment before proceeding with many dental treatments. Although there are very few outright medical contraindications to receiving dental care, certain factors may increase the risk of poor outcomes, including morbidity and mortality. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 5 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التَّعَب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that patients who experience fatigue often also report significant anxious feelings. Clinically, fatigue is also associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي), suggesting that the presence of persistent tiredness may coincide with or signal an underlying yeast infection of the mouth. Recognizing the link between fatigue, anxiety, and oral candidiasis can aid clinicians in evaluating patients holistically, prompting assessment for both psychological distress and possible infectious contributors when fatigue is reported.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier “rehabilitace” is applicable as a therapeutic approach for the conditions قرحة قلاعية, الالتصاق, and اِضطِراب اللسان, indicating that rehabilitation can be employed in the management of these oral health disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: or discectomy. In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both anxiety (القلق) and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Anxiety can manifest as vague chest discomfort, making it an important psychosomatic contributor to this symptom. Oral candidiasis, a fungal infection of the mouth, may also be linked to nonspecific chest pain, potentially through systemic effects, medication side‑effects, or underlying immunosuppression that predisposes patients to both conditions. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may signal either anxiety or oral thrush—or a combination of both—helps clinicians pursue a more comprehensive evaluation and tailor management to address the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2836
retrieved_docs.content: التَّعَب is a clinical symptom that is commonly associated with تضيق الأبهر and also with داء المبيضات الفموي. In patients with تضيق الأبهر, fatigue often reflects reduced cardiac output and may indicate disease severity, prompting evaluation of valve function and hemodynamic status. Similarly, fatigue can manifest in داء المبيضات الفموي, reflecting systemic effects of the oral Candida infection or related nutritional deficiencies, and may guide clinicians to consider antifungal therapy and supportive care. Recognizing fatigue as a shared clinical feature of these distinct conditions supports more accurate differential diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium supplements (مكملات الكالسيوم) are intended for therapeutic use, meaning they are administered to treat or prevent medical conditions related to calcium deficiency or bone health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.
